text: the King of Elleron standing beyond Utak at his end of the table. He tilted his chin up when he caught Aurel’s look. “Is Rickard dead?” “He is.” Aurel rested his wounded hand on the table. “We know the truth of matters now. Tregard demands recompense.” Ergant huffed. “Your king is dead. This arbitration is over.” He reached for the already-agreed-upon papers, signed by both kings, but Aurel said, “Shall we pay T’giskall another visit?” Ergant stilled and Utak shot Aurel a disapproving glance. She knew that he would have to touch Ergant to pull him into the mirror realm, but she said nothing. Ergant’s hand trembled, but he snatched it back. “You are merely a mirrorwright. You cannot speak for Tregard!” “We are all equals here. Here, I am king. And no one can leave until both sides come to an agreement.” Ergant glowered, expression pinched, as if he were chewing on something bitter. “What is it that you want?” “Your southern isles, for a start.” Aurel turned, his hand sliding from the table, leaving behind a handprint and smear of blood. He shifted to the Tregard end of the table, faced Ergant again across its length. Utak remained at its center. “The current locations of these pirate ships you’ve hired as well. I’m certain we can reach an agreement on additional terms.” He sat, reached for the quill, eyes never leaving Ergant’s face. “Shall we parley?” “The hardest working writer in horror” is a phrase which is tossed around much, (maybe too much), but I can't think of another writer who embodies this better than Mark Allan Gunnells. Every time I log onto Facebook, it seems like Mark has either won another flash fiction contest, or published another book, or sold another story. His passion for horror and storytelling is undeniable, and is evident in every single thing he writes, as it is in his offering for this anthology, “Phoenix.” PHOENIX by Mark Allan Gunnells "Look at that,” Sheryl said, sticking her arm past Frank as she pointed emphatically out the driver’s side window. Frank jerked, causing the car to swerve to the left before he pulled it back in line. “Honey, are you trying to make me drive us off the road? Why don’t you just punch me in the neck and grab the steering wheel next time?” “Stop being so melodramatic and look .” Frank followed her pointing finger just as they passed a narrow dirt road that disappeared into a wooded area. Next to the road was a large sign that read: PHOENIX DRIVE-IN OPEN NIGHTLY AT 7 TONIGHT’S DOUBLE FEATURE EAST OF EDEN & REBEL WITHOUT A CAUSE Frank just grunted as the sign disappeared behind them. “What are you doing, turn around,” Sheryl said. “For what?” “It’s 6:50, we can just make the first show.” “Surely you’re not serious.” “I surely am, and my name isn’t Shirley.” “That’s not how that quote goes, you know.” “Whatever. I’ve never been to a drive-in before.” “Well, I have. My parents used to take me and my little brother to a drive-in when we were kids, and it wasn’t that much fun. Give me a nice roomy theater with comfy stadium seating any day.” “Oh, come on Frank, be a sport. I’ve always wanted to go to a drive-in but I didn’t think there were any left.” “Look, you wanted to take the scenic route back from your Aunt Belinda’s instead of the interstate which would have gotten us home much quicker, and I said fine, but I am not going to stop on the way and watch two movies. It’s going to be after midnight before we get home as it is.” “Well, we don’t have to stay for both films, we can just watch the first one then take the interstate the rest of the way.” “Sheryl, I’m really not in the mood for a movie.” “But it’s James Dean, you love James Dean.” “Yeah, and I have all his movies on Blu-ray back at the house.” “But that’s not the same as seeing it on the big screen. It’ll be worth it, trust me. I’ll make it worth your while.” “What are you talking about?” “I’ve always heard that drive-ins could be a lot of … fun for teenagers.” “Sheryl, we’re not teenagers.” “We could be tonight.” Frank looked over at his wife with a frown, as if he wasn’t sure who was sitting next to him and how she’d gotten in his car. “What are getting at?” Sheryl scooted closer, placing a hand on Frank’s thigh. “You know that thing you like, the thing I’m rarely in the mood for? Well, I just might be in the mood for it tonight.” “Are you serious?” “We could park somewhere near the back of the lot…might be exciting.” “What’s gotten into you?” Sheryl let her hand stray further up her husband’s thigh then said teasingly, “What, not interested?” Frank was interested all right, and if Sheryl’s hand went much higher she’d find out just how interested he really was, but he decided to tease her back just a bit. “Oh, I don’t know. I’d be all messy, I’d hate to have to drive all the way back home like that.” Leaning forward, Sheryl whispered in his ear, “There wouldn’t necessarily be any mess.” “You don’t mean…?” She nodded. “ If we stay for both movies.” Frank didn’t say anything, but he flipped on his signal when he spotted a BP gas station up ahead on the right. He turned around in the parking lot then headed back toward the Phoenix. # The ticket booth was a couple of yards down the dirt road on the left. Behind the glass was a teenage girl with a blonde ponytail and dead eyes. She didn’t seem to notice the car at first, and when she did she seemed surprised, as if not expecting any more customers this close to show time. She slid open the window, stared
